coredata.dc:title: Flower-like silver bismuthate supported on nitrogen-doped carbon dots modified graphene oxide sheets with excellent degradation activity for organic pollutants
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yue, Xiaoyang
Miao, Xuli
Shen, Xiaoping
Ji, Zhenyuan
Zhou, Hu
Sun, Yiming
Xu, Keqiang
Zhu, Guoxing
Kong, Lirong
Chen, Quanrun
Li, Na
He, Xiaomei
coredata.dc:description:
In this study, a new ternary AgBiO3/GO/NCDs composite (GO = graphene oxide, NCDs = nitrogen-doped carbon dots) has been successfully prepared through in-situ growth of flower-like AgBiO3 on GO/NCDs complex support. The AgBiO3/GO/NCDs composite exhibits significantly enhanced degradation activities towards organic pollutants of rhodamine B, phenol and tetracycline. Especially, the refractory tetracycline (20 mg L−1) can be completely removed within 6.0 min with a dosage of 30 mg of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs under the assistance of peroxymonosulfate (PMS, 0.2 mM). It is revealed that GO in the composite can facilitate the quick and efficient electron transfer and improve the generation of reactive oxygen species during the degradation process, while the NCDs may play double roles as both the electron-acceptor and the reactive site. Besides, the electrons can be captured by PMS to produce plenty of sulfate radicals (SO4
−) with very strong oxidation ability. All these factors collaboratively promote the degradation efficiency of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs towards organic pollutants. The excellent degradation activities of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs endow it with potential application in wastewater purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AgBiO3
Graphene oxide
Carbon dots
Degradation
Organic pollutants

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of AgBiO3, AgBiO3/GO and AgBiO3/GO/NCDs and (b) Raman spectra of the as-prepared samples.
SEM and TEM images of (a, b) AgBiO3, (c) AgBiO3/GO, and (d-g) AgBiO3/GO/NCDs. (h) HRTEM image of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) O 1s, (c) C 1s, (d) Ag 3d, (e) Bi 4f and (f) N 1s for the AgBiO3/GO/NCDs.

The degradation of RhB based on the as-prepared samples under the condition of (a) without and (b) with 2.0 mM PMS; Pseudo-first-order kinetic curves of the degradation of RhB over different samples (c) without and (d) with 2.0 mM PMS; (e) The degradation of RhB over AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 with different amounts of PMS; (f) The time-dependent TOC removal ratio for the degradation of RhB with AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2. ([RhB] = 10 mg/L, [PMS] = 2 mM).

The degradation of (a) phenol and (b) tetracycline based on the as-prepared samples; (c) The degradation of tetracycline based on the as-prepared samples and 2.0 mM PMS. ([phenol] = 25 mg/L, [TC] = 20 mg/L, [PMS] = 2 mM).

The effect of (a–c) initial pollutant concentrations on the degradation rates. The effect of (d–f) pH values and (g–i) the used amounts of catalysts on the degradation ratios of RhB (90 min), phenol (15 min) and TC (12.5 min).

(a) ESR spectra of (1) TEMP-1O2, (2) DMPO-O2
−, (3) DMPOX and (4) DMPO-SO4
− adducts in the presence of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2. (1–3) were tested in aqueous solutions and (4) in methanol (Conditions: [Sample] = 0.5 g/L, [PMS] = 2 mM, [TEMP] = 40 mM, [DMPO] = 25 mM, without irradiation). (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (Nyquist plots) of the as-prepared samples (Conditions: [Sample] = 4 g/L, Solution: mixture of 980 μL of ethanol and 20 μL of Nafion, Frequency range: 105–0.1 Hz).
The possible degradation mechanism of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs composites.

head.ce:title: Flower-like silver bismuthate supported on nitrogen-doped carbon dots modified graphene oxide sheets with excellent degradation activity for organic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study, a new ternary AgBiO3/GO/NCDs composite (GO = graphene oxide, NCDs = nitrogen-doped carbon dots) has been successfully prepared through in-situ growth of flower-like AgBiO3 on GO/NCDs complex support. The AgBiO3/GO/NCDs composite exhibits significantly enhanced degradation activities towards organic pollutants of rhodamine B, phenol and tetracycline. Especially, the refractory tetracycline (20 mg L−1) can be completely removed within 6.0 min with a dosage of 30 mg of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs under the assistance of peroxymonosulfate (PMS, 0.2 mM). It is revealed that GO in the composite can facilitate the quick and efficient electron transfer and improve the generation of reactive oxygen species during the degradation process, while the NCDs may play double roles as both the electron-acceptor and the reactive site. Besides, the electrons can be captured by PMS to produce plenty of sulfate radicals (SO4
−) with very strong oxidation ability. All these factors collaboratively promote the degradation efficiency of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs towards organic pollutants. The excellent degradation activities of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs endow it with potential application in wastewater purification.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industrialization, the environmental pollution has arisen as an urgent issue of social concerns, especially on the disposal of organic wastewater [1]. The organic pollutants such as dyestuff, aromatic nitro compounds, phenols and antibiotics have a serious impact on the ecosystems and human health because of their toxicity and carcinogenicity [2–3]. Rhodamine B (RhB) is an organic dye widely used in textile industry, and also an important organic contaminant in environment [4]. Phenolic compounds have high toxicity and stability as well as low biodegradability in water, which pose a potential threat to human health. Among phenolic compounds, phenol is more difficult to degrade than chlorinated and nitro-substituted phenols [5,6]. Tetracycline (TC), as a typical antibiotic, has a wide application in human and animal medicine. However, due to its poor adsorbance in digestive tract of animals, quite large amount (about 50–80%) of TC is excreted into the environment [7]. As a result, how to deal with RhB, phenol and TC in the environment has become a growing concern. Many advanced oxidation technologies have been developed to solve this problem, such as Fenton reaction, electro-Fenton system, photocatalysis, ozone sonolysis, catalytic oxidation, supercritical water oxidation, radiolysis, ultrasonic irradiation and so on [8,9]. Nevertheless, these advanced oxidation processes come across a big challenge towards the practical application, especially on the disposal of polluted groundwater.

As we known, the reactive oxygen species (ROS), including singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anion radical (
O2 −), hydroxyl radical (
OH) and sulfate radicals (SO4
−), play a vital role in oxidative degradation of organic pollutants due to the high reactivity of ROS [10]. Silver bismuthate (AgBiO3) has recently received much attention because it can self-produce large amounts of ROS without light illumination or any additional oxidant due to the controllable release of lattice oxygen [11]. The similar phenomenon was also observed over Bi(V)/Bi(III) composites, which can directly degrade bisphenol A [12]. However, the high price and low degradation efficiency of AgBiO3 limit its practical application for wastewater treatment.

Graphene oxide, as a typical derivative of graphene with various oxygen-containing groups, has been widely used as an ideal support for various functional materials due to its two-dimensional plane structure, large specific surface area, high mobility of charge carrier and good dispersibility in aqueous solution [13–16]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs), as a new zero-dimensional carbon material, also attract great attention due to its excellent electron reservoir and electron transfer properties. Moreover, the properties of CDs can be further optimized by doping with other elements such as N [17–18] and S [19] because the electron structure of CDs can be significantly affected by doping [20]. Due to the function of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) as electron acceptor, they have been used as co-catalyst for the construction of electron surface separation centers in a variety of applications [21–23].

Peroxymonosulfate (PMS) has been used as donors of the reactive species in photocatalytic processes because it can be activated by the photogenerated electrons to generate sulfate radical (SO4
−), which has a more positive potential (2.5–3.1 V) than
OH (1.8–2.7 V), and thus possesses stronger oxidizing capability compared to
OH [24–25]. For instance, Zhu et al. reported that Co3O4 modified g-C3N4 composites show enhanced degradation of diclofenac sodium after the addition of PMS [26].

In this work, a new ternary AgBiO3/GO/NCDs composite has been successfully prepared through in-situ growth of flower-like AgBiO3 on GO/NCDs complex support. In the composite, GO acts as the support material, promoting the formation of flower-like AgBiO3 and providing more reactive sites. And the NCDs anchored on the surface of GO serve as both the electron acceptor and reaction site. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of the different components, the AgBiO3/GO/NCDs composite shows significantly enhanced degradation performance for RhB, phenol and TC. The possible degradation mechanism is also proposed.

Natural flake graphite (45 µm, 99.95%), potassium peroxymonosulfate (Oxone, 2KHSO5·KHSO4·K2SO4) and 1,5-diphenylcarbazide (DPC, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd., China. Other chemicals adopted in this experiment were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used through the experiment. A modified Hummers method was adopted for the preparation of graphite oxide [27], which was then ultrasonically dispersed in DI water to obtain the GO dispersion (1.0 mg mL−1) for further use.

Preparation of NCDs: The NCDs aqueous dispersion was synthesized according to a previously reported method [28]. Typically, citrate acid (0.96 g) was dissolved in a certain volume of water (10 mL) containing 335 µL of ethylenediamine, which was then transferred to a 25 mL of Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. NCDs were obtained by freeze-drying after a dialysis process for 24 h to remove the impurities. NCDs aqueous dispersion (1.0 mg mL−1) was prepared by re-dispersing the obtained NCDs in water and then stored at 4 °C for future use.

Preparation of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs: In a typical synthesis, 85 mg of AgNO3 and a certain volume of NCDs aqueous dispersion (1.0 mg mL−1) were mixed with 1.8 mL of GO aqueous dispersion (1.0 mg mL−1). Afterwards, DI water was added until the total volume reached to 45.0 mL. After stirred for 24 h, the resulting mixture was heated at 70 °C, and then 158 mg of NaBiO3 was added in. The reaction system was stirred for another 0.5 h. Finally, the product was collected by centrifugation, washed three times with DI water and ethanol, respectively, and then dried at 45 °C in vacuum for 24 h. The obtained samples were denoted as AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-1, AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 and AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-3 for the NCDs feeding amount of 0.18, 0.90 and 1.80 mL, respectively. For comparison, pure AgBiO3 sample was prepared by directly mixing AgNO3 and NaBiO3 aqueous solutions under the same condition in the absence of GO and NCDs. And AgBiO3/GO samples were also prepared in the absence of NCDs, and denoted as AgBiO3/GO-1, AgBiO3/GO-2, AgBiO3/GO-3 and AgBiO3/GO-4 for the GO feeding amount of 0.18, 0.90, 1.80 and 3.60 mL, respectively. The preparation process of the AgBiO3/GO/NCDs composites is depicted in Scheme 1 .

The phase structures of the as-synthesized samples were performed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) at a scanning rate of 7° min−1. Raman scattering was investigated on a DXR Raman spectrometer using a 532 nm laser source. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer. The morphologies and microstructures of the samples were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6480). The composition of the products was estimated by energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDX attached to SEM). The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of radicals spin-trapped by spin-trap reagents 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were measured on a Bruker EPR A300-10/12 spectrometer. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was measured at 77 K using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 apparatus and porosity analyzer. Elemental analysis was performed on an element analyzer (FLASH1112A) and the metal ions contents in the composites were determined by an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Vista-MPX). Total organic carbon (TOC) was examined using a TOC analyzer (Analytik Jena AG multi N/C 2100). The following expression on the TOC percent removal rate was used to evaluate the degree of mineralization:
Mineralization =
TOC 0
-
TOC t
/
TOC 0
where TOC0 is the concentration of TOC at t = 0 and TOCt is its value at degradation time t (min).

The degradations of RhB, phenol and tetracycline were carried out to evaluate the degradation activities of the as-synthesized samples. The experiments were performed on a GHX-3 photochemical reactor (Science and Education Equipment Co. Ltd., Yangzhou, China). In a typical degradation experiment of RhB, 30 mg of the as-prepared samples was dispersed in 60 mL of 10 mg L−1 RhB aqueous solution. While for phenol and tetracycline degradation, 30 mg of the samples was dispersed in 30 mL of phenol solution (25 mg L−1) or tetracycline solution (20 mg L−1). All the experiments were conducted under constant stirring at room temperature. During the degradation process, regular intervals were recorded to withdraw 3 mL of the suspension, which was then centrifuged. An UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to determine the residual concentration of the pollutant by analyzing the supernatant. The characteristic absorption wavelengths of RhB, phenol and tetracycline are 554, 270 and 358 nm, respectively. For recycling experiments, the samples after degradation were collected by centrifugation, and then were re-dispersed into fresh pollutant solution for the next round of degradation. The degradation ratio (DR) was calculated from: DR% = 100 × (C0 − Ct
)/C 0 , where C0 (mg/L) is the initial concentration of pollutants, Ct (mg/L) is the residual pollutant concentration at reaction time t (min). All experiments were carried out in duplicate and only the mean values were reported.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed in a frequency range from 105 to 0.1 Hz on a 760E electrochemical analyzer (ChenHua Instruments, Shanghai, China) using a standard three-electrode system. The saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and platinum wire were used as reference electrodes and counter electrodes, respectively. The electrolyte used in this system was 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. A polished glass carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter) deposited with the as-prepared sample was used as the working electrode. In detail, the working electrode was prepared as follows: First, 4 mg of the as-prepared samples was dispersed in a mixed solution of 980 μL of ethanol and 20 μL of Nafion by sonication to form a homogeneous suspension, afterwards 5.0 μL of the solution was drop-cast on the polished GCE, which was dried naturally.

The XRD patterns of the as-synthesized AgBiO3, AgBiO3/GO and AgBiO3/GO/NCDs are shown in Fig. 1 a. For bare AgBiO3, all the diffraction peaks can be well indexed to hexagonal phase AgBiO3 (JCPDS No. 89-9072) [11]. AgBiO3/GO and AgBiO3/GO/NCDs both exhibit similar XRD patterns with pure AgBiO3, and no diffraction peaks from NCDs and GO were observed, which could be ascribed to the low contents as well as the poor crystallinity of GO and NCDs as revealed by the wide diffraction peaks of single NCDs and GO. The particle size of AgBiO3 can be roughly evaluated using the Debye-Scherrer equation, D = 0.9λ/(βcosθ), where D is the crystalline size, λ is the incident X-ray wavelength (0.154 nm), β is the half-width and θ is the diffraction angle. The average particle size of AgBiO3 in AgBiO3/GO/NCDs is estimated to be in the range of 31–33 nm. Raman spectra were recorded to further evaluate the introduction of NCDs and GO (Fig. 1b). The ternary composite of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs shows two discernable peaks at 1350 and 1608 cm−1, which corresponds to the D and G bands of carbon-based materials [13], well consistent with the existence of NCDs and GO in the composite.

Fig. 2 shows the SEM and TEM images of the as-synthesized samples. The pure AgBiO3 shows a sheet-like morphology and a great deal of stacking has occurred in its structure (Fig. 2a and b). After the addition of GO, the morphology can be adjusted to flower-like structure assembled from nanosheets (Fig. 2c). An appropriate amount of GO in the binary composites is crucial to achieving this flower-like structure (Fig. S1, Supporting Information), and the optimal loading amount of GO is about 1.0 wt% (AgBiO3/GO-3). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2d, the introduction of NCDs can still maintain the flower-like morphology. Therefore, after modified by NCDs via π-π interaction, GO still plays its role as a support for the formation of the flower-like structured AgBiO3, which makes it expose more active sites. TME images also exhibit a nanosheet-assembled flower-like structure (Fig. 2e, f). In addition, the formation of AgBiO3 is further confirmed by HRTEM (Fig. 2h), in which the clear lattice fringes with the spacings of 0.31 and 0.23 nm correspond to the (1 0 4) and (2 0 2) lattice planes of AgBiO3, respectively. The intimate interface contact can be clearly seen between AgBiO3 and GO, indicating the effective combination of AgBiO3 and GO (Fig. 2h). No obvious carbon layers can be observed surrounding the AgBiO3 particles in the TEM and HRTEM images, indicating that the AgBiO3 particles were not encapsulated by GO sheets. However, it is possible that some of NCDs were encapsulated by GO sheets due to the tiny size of NCDs.

XPS measurement was further carried out to clarify the surface composition and chemical states of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs. The C, N, O, Ag and Bi elements were detected in AgBiO3/GO/NCDs from the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3 a), which is in good consistent with the composition of the composite. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) exhibits five deconvoluted peaks at 529.4, 530.4, 530.9, 531.6 and 533.2 eV, which can be ascribed to BiO, AgO, OH, CO and CO bonds, respectively. The BiO and AgO bonds correspond to the O2− in AgBiO3, while the OH bond corresponds to the OH functional groups from both GO and NCDs and/or the H2O molecules adsorbed on the surface of the sample [29]. The CO and CO peaks originate from the NCDs and GO in the composite [28–29]. For C1s spectrum (Fig. 3c), the deconvoluted peaks at 284.8, 286.6, 287.8 and 288.5 eV can be ascribed to CC, CO, CO and NCN bonds, respectively [30–31]. The high-resolution Ag 3d spectrum is shown in Fig. 3d, the Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 peaks at 367.4 eV and 373.4 eV can be ascribed to Ag+ in AgBiO3. The high-resolution Bi 4f spectrum (Fig. 3e) exhibits two peaks at 158.5 and 163.8 eV, which correspond to the Bi5+ in AgBiO3 [11]. For N1s spectrum (Fig. 3f), a weak peak appears at around 400 eV, suggesting the successful anchoring of NCDs in AgBiO3/GO/NCDs though the N content is very low [29]. Besides, the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (Fig. S2) also confirms the existence of C, N, O, Ag and Bi elements in AgBiO3/GO/NCDs. Considering that these measurements are semi-quantitative, the actual compositions of the samples were further examined by ICP and elemental analysis. The contents of C, N, Ag, Bi from ICP and elemental analysis are shown in Table S1, which are well consistent with the feeding mass ratios of AgBiO3: GO: NCDs (100:1:0.5) in the AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 synthesis.

From the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. S3), the BET specific surface areas of AgBiO3 and AgBiO3/GO/NCDs were determined to be 12.71 and 16.38 m2/g, respectively, which shows an improvement of 28.87% for AgBiO3/GO/NCDs compared to the bare AgBiO3, indicating that the introduction of GO and NCDs into AgBiO3 can improve the specific surface area, which would provide more active sites for the adsorption and promote the degradation activities.

RhB, phenol and tetracycline were chosen as the model contaminants to evaluate the degradation activity of the as-prepared samples. The results of RhB degradation are shown in Figs. 4 and S4a. As shown in Fig. 4a, compared with single AgBiO3, all the binary AgBiO3/GO composites show enhanced degradation activities towards RhB, indicating that the degradation performance of AgBiO3 can be significantly improved by the introduction of GO. Among the binary composites, the AgBiO3/GO-3 shows the highest degradation activity for RhB with a degradation rate of about 97.94% within 90 min, which is much higher than 80.02% of pure AgBiO3 (Fig. 4a). With the increasing amount of GO, the degradation efficiency of AgBiO3/GO composites increases first and then decreases, which can be ascribed to the fact that too high loading amount of GO covers the active sites and thus restrains the ROS process. By introducing appropriate amount of NCDs into the AgBiO3/GO binary system, the degradation activity can be further improved. Specifically, the AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 exhibits a degradation efficiency of 99.37% within 90 min, higher than 97.94% of the corresponding binary AgBiO3/GO-3. The binary AgBiO3/GO-3 and ternary AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 samples with optimal performances would be used for the further degradation research hereinafter. Furthermore, the degradation properties of the as-prepared samples were also evaluated in the presence of PMS (Fig. 4b). Obviously, after the introduction of 2.0 mM PMS, the degradation performances of all the samples are greatly improved, and the AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2+PMS shows the highest degradation efficiency of 95.35% within 10 min. In contrast, the single PMS shows a degradation efficiency of only 8.50%. The result can be mainly ascribed to the activation of PMS by the electrons produced in ROS process to generate highly active SO4
− [26]. From this perspective, the presence of GO and NCDs makes it possible to provide a larger specific surface area and more reaction sites, so that the HSO5 − could more effectively contact with the composites, and thus was converted to SO4
− more quickly. As a result, the enhanced degradation activity was achieved. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the degradation reaction rate can be described by the pseudo-first-order kinetic: −ln(C/C 0) = kt, where t (min) is the degradation time and k (min−1) is the apparent reaction rate constant. The effect of GO, NCDs and PMS on the degradation activity of the composites is also reflected by the apparent rate constants (Fig. S5a and b). For the degradation of RhB without PMS, AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 possesses a rate constant of 0.05519 min−1, which is about 3 times of that of AgBiO3 (0.01845 min−1). After the introduction of PMS into the degradation systems, the ternary composite system of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2+PMS possesses the highest rate constant of 0.2909 min−1, which is almost 5.3 times of that of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 without PMS. In addition, four successive cycles of RhB degradation were performed to evaluate the durability of the samples (Fig. S6a). Because the ROS process is a successive consumption process of the sample itself, its performance worsened gradually as the cycle experiments proceeded. However, it can be clearly seen that the durability and degradation efficiency of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 are still higher than those of the bare AgBiO3. Meanwhile, the degradation experiments of RhB over AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 were also carried out by adding different amount of PMS. As shown in Fig. 4e, with the increasing amount of PMS, the degradation efficiency was correspondingly improved. Furthermore, XRD pattern of the AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 after four cycles was shown in Fig. S6b. The new distinguished peaks at 2 θ = 23.90, 30.24 and 52.23° can be well indexed to the (1 0 1), (1 0 3) and (1 1 6) crystal planes of Bi2(CO3)O2. TEM images (Fig. S7a–c) of the AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 after four cycles indicate that though some collapse occurred in the flower-like structure, it was still assembled by nanosheets. In addition, the formation of Bi2(CO3)O2 was also confirmed by HRTEM. As shown in Fig. S7d, the clear lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.228 nm can be indexed to the (0 0 6) lattice plane of Bi2(CO3)O2, except that the lattice spacing of 0.268 nm corresponds to the (0 0 6) lattice plane of pristine AgBiO3. XPS measurement of the AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 after four cycles was also performed to comprehend the transformation from AgBiO3 into Bi2(CO3)O2 (Fig. S8). The C, N, O, Ag and Bi elements were also detected in the used AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 (Fig. S8a). However, compared to the fresh sample, the used one shows weakened Ag peak and strengthened C peak, which can be ascribed to the dissolution of Ag+ and the formation of Bi2(CO3)O2 during the degradation process [11]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S8b) exhibits that the Ag-O peak is obviously weakened, further confirming the dissolution of Ag+. The Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 peaks have a small shift about 0.2 eV to higher binding energies (367.4 and 373.4 eV, respectively) after four cycles because of the broken electrostatic balance (Fig. S8c) [11]. For the high-resolution Bi 4f spectra, the peaks of Bi 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 in the used AgBiO3/GO/NCDs can be well deconvoluted into two bimodal peaks at binding energies of 158.6 and 159.8 eV for Bi 4f7/2, and 163.9 and 165.0 eV for Bi 4f5/2, confirming that Bi5+ is partially reduced to Bi3+ accompanied by ROS process (Fig. S8d) [11]. These results are in good consistent with the XRD result, further confirming the formation of Bi2(CO3)O2. For further evaluating the degradation performance, TOC experiment was performed to assess contaminant mineralization ability. As shown in Fig. 4f, AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 exhibits a TOC removal rate of 89.01% within 90 min, indicating that mineralization process occurred in the degradation of RhB, and RhB could be effectively degraded by AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2.

Furthermore, the degradation ability of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs towards the refractory phenol and tetracycline was also investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. S4b–c. As shown in Fig. 5a and b, compared to the bare AgBiO3 and binary AgBiO3/GO, the ternary AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 shows higher degradation efficiency for the degradation of phenol and tetracycline. About 97.22% of phenol and 97.80% of tetracycline can be degraded within 15 and 12.5 min, respectively. When introducing PMS into the system, the degradation activity for tetracycline was greatly enhanced, and the tetracycline can be completely degraded within 6.0 min (Fig. 5c). However, due to the disturbance of PMS on the absorbance of phenol, the degradation efficiency for phenol cannot be accurately measured after the addition of PMS. Based on the above results, it can be concluded that the introduction of GO and NCDs can significantly improve the degradation activity of AgBiO3, and appropriate loading amounts of GO and NCDs are also important to optimize the degradation performance. A comparison between the as-prepared AgBiO3/GO/NCDs and the reported state-of-the-art Bi-based catalysts is listed in Table S2, from which it can be seen that AgBiO3/GO/NCDs in this work shows superior or comparable degradation performance to the reported Bi-based catalysts. Moreover, besides the degradation for traditional pollutants (RhB and phenol), the as-prepared AgBiO3/GO/NCDs also shows excellent degradation activity towards TC (a typical antibiotic), which extends its application for wastewater treatment. Especially, the degradation performance can be further improved by the addition of PMS, and the rate constant of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs+PMS for RhB degradation is as high as 0.2909 min−1, which is much higher than the reported Bi-based catalysts.

The effect of some parameters on the degradation efficiency was also investigated, including initial pollutant concentration, pH value, and the used amount of catalysts [32,33]. As shown in Fig. 6 a–c, the initial pollutant concentration has an effect on the degradation efficiency. With the increase of the initial pollutant concentrations, the degradation of the pollutants needs longer time. It is worth noting that the initial degradation rate is relatively low for high concentration RhB solution, which can be mainly ascribed to the excessive dye molecules adsorbed on the surface of catalyst particles [34]. The effect of pH values on the degradation ratio (DR) of pollutants was studied in the pH range of 2–12 with HCl and NaOH solutions as adjusting reagents. As shown in Fig. 6a–c, when the pH value was adjusted to 12, the degradation ratio decreased to 11.74% (RhB), 55.13% (phenol) and 77.55% (TC), respectively, which has a corresponding drop of 87.63%, 42.09% and 21.02% compared to that without pH adjustment. In addition, in acidic condition, it was more difficult to degrade phenol than RhB and TC due to the high stability of phenol in acidic medium. As for the effect of the amount of catalysts, as shown in Fig. 6g–i, the degradation ratio increases firstly and then keeps stable with the increasing amount of the catalysts, which is consistent with the related degradation processes reported previously [35,36].”

To clarify the degradation mechanism, the ESR measurements of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 were carried out. The spin trap of TEMP is used to capture 1O2, which shows a typical three-peak spectrum in Fig. 7 a (1), indicating that the stable radicals of TEMP-1O2 are generated through this trapping reaction [31,37]. In addition, the unexpected DMPO-O2
− (Fig. 7a (2)) and DMPOX (Fig. 7a (3)) (an oxidized form of DMPO) spectra are observed in measuring O2
− and
OH by the spin trap of DMPO, which is ascribed to the influence of 1O2, and the similar phenomenon has also been reported in previous literature [37]. As for the detection of the SO4
−, the two apparent new signals of DMPO-SO4
− adducts marked by diamond symbols in Fig. 7a (4) appeared after the addition of PMS as compared with the DMPOX spectrum, which demonstrates the formation of SO4
− [38]. It is worth noting that it is the formation of DMPOX that affects the detection of DMPO-SO4
− adducts, which shows six typical signals in the normal condition. EIS measurement was carried out to validate the improved electron transfer behavior of the as-prepared samples. The Nyquist plots of the AgBiO3, AgBiO3/GO-3 and AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 photoelectrodes were presented in Fig. 7b. The semicircle part at the high frequency region corresponds to the charge-transfer process at the photoelectrode interface, and a smaller radius implies more efficient charge transfer [29]. Compared to that both of AgBiO3 and AgBiO3/GO-3, the smaller arc of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 implies that the interfacial electron transfer at the electrode interface has been accelerated by modification of GO and NCDs.

In accordance with the above results, a possible degradation mechanism for AgBiO3/GO/NCDs is proposed in Fig. 8 . Owing to the controllable release of lattice oxygen from the crystalline AgBiO3, considerable reactive oxygen species (ROS) could be self-produced by the AgBiO3 without light illumination or any additional oxidant [11]. Along with the ROS process, the electrons except for a small part participate in the inevitable reduction of Bi5+, will transfer to the surface of GO due to the good electrical conductivity and the close contact between GO and AgBiO3, and then partially be captured by the NCDs, not further reducing Ag+ any more. Besides, more active sites exposed by the flower-like structure could also effectively capture the pollutant molecules. Previous literature has pointed out that the electrons on the graphene surface have a strong reduction ability in the specific edge zone [39–40]. Therefore, the electrons transferred to the graphene surface directly act as a reducing agent for the production of
O2 − only at the edges of the graphene. After anchoring the NCDs on the surface of GO, some electrons are trapped in the NCDs, which are considered as reaction sites for further degradation of the pollutants. Therefore, the NCDs anchored on the surface of GO provide more reaction sites for the samples, and the electrons free on the surface of GO are efficiently utilized, which is in good agreement with the above results. When PMS is added into the degradation system, the accumulated electrons will firstly convert PMS to sulfate radicals, whose oxidation potential is stronger than that of
OH, making the reaction more efficient. As a result, the degradation efficiency was significantly enhanced.

To further confirm the proposed mechanism, the reduction experiment of toxic Cr (VI) was carried out. As shown in Fig. S9, the reduction of Cr (VI) with the AgBiO3/GO-3 and AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 reaches about 67% within 10 min, however, it takes 20 min for the bare AgBiO3 to reach the same level. Obviously, AgBiO3/GO-3 and AgBiO3/GO/NCDs-2 both possess the ability of quick electron transfer, and thus Cr (VI) was reduced more quickly. That is, the quick and efficient electron transfer was achieved between AgBiO3 and GO, which is in good consistent with our degradation mechanism.

In this study, AgBiO3/GO/NCDs ternary composites have been successfully prepared through in-situ growth of flower-like AgBiO3 on GO/NCDs complex support. AgBiO3/GO/NCDs shows significantly enhanced performances towards the degradation of RhB, phenol and tetracycline, especially after the addition of PMS. The rate constant of AgBiO3/GO/NCDs+PMS for RhB degradation is as high as 0.2909 min−1, which is 15.8 and 5.3 times higher than that of AgBiO3 (0.01845 min−1) and AgBiO3/GO/NCDs (0.05519 min−1). The excellent degradation performance can be ascribed to more active sites exposed by the flower-like structure, the quick electron transfer originated from the intimate interface contact between GO and AgBiO3 as well as the electrons with strong reduction ability in the electron-acceptor NCDs. Besides, the introduction of PMS can capture the electrons to produce plenty of SO4
− with stronger oxidation potential than
OH, further promoting the degradation efficiency. This study demonstrates that the degradation performance of AgBiO3 towards organic contaminants can be greatly improved by the construction of suitable multicomponent systems, and the AgBiO3/GO/NCDs is a promising candidate for the practical organic pollutant treatment, especially for the refractory phenol and tetracycline.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu province (Nos. BK20171295 and BK20150507), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21875091 and 51602129).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
